skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Create, edit, analyze, and validate Excel workbooks using pandas, openpyxl, and LibreOffice formula recalculation.
skill.summary: Build and verify spreadsheet files with formulas, formatting, analysis, and recalculation support.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-severity patterns, but the C2, weak cryptography, hardcoded URL, and markdown backtick alerts are false positives in spreadsheet documentation. The confirmed risks are legitimate but sensitive behavior: recalc.py writes a LibreOffice macro into the user profile and runs external LibreOffice and timeout commands. Publish with a clear warning about local file-system changes and external command execution.

medium_findings.title: Persistent LibreOffice Macro Installation
medium_findings.description: recalc.py creates a LibreOffice macro under the user's LibreOffice profile and writes Module1.xba. This is intentional for recalculation, but it modifies persistent application configuration outside the workbook directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly builds a LibreOffice profile path and writes a StarBasic module file. The behavior is clear, but the macro body only recalculates, saves, and closes the active document.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution for Formula Recalculation
medium_findings.description: recalc.py invokes soffice and timeout or gtimeout through subprocess.run. Arguments are passed as lists without shell=True, so shell injection is not evident, but the skill still executes local programs on user-supplied workbook files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and confirmed. Risk is moderated because command names are fixed and the workbook path is supplied as one argument rather than interpolated into a shell command.

low_findings.title: Unbounded Workbook Cell Scanning
low_findings.description: After recalculation, recalc.py iterates through all cells in all sheets to find formula errors and count formulas. Very large or adversarial workbooks could cause high CPU or memory use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full-sheet iteration is explicit and there are no row or cell limits. The impact is availability-related and depends on workbook size.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URLs Are Legal References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs in LICENSE.txt point to Anthropic legal terms, and the URL-like text in recalc.py is an XML namespace. No runtime network request or data exfiltration path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static text or XML metadata, not HTTP client calls. I did not find evidence of network access in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: C2 and Cryptography Keywords
low_findings.description: The C2 alert maps to an Excel cell reference in a formula example, and the weak cryptography alerts map to ordinary spreadsheet analysis terms. No command-and-control behavior or cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings appear inside spreadsheet examples and documentation. There is no evidence of encoded payloads, beaconing, or cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Are Documentation
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md backtick alerts come from fenced code examples and inline formatting. They are not Ruby or shell backtick execution in a runtime script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged spans are examples or inline literals. I did not find executable Ruby backtick syntax in a source file.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Invokes Local Office Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The script uses subprocess.run to initialize LibreOffice, check gtimeout, and run the recalculation command. This is expected for the skill but should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are confirmed and security-relevant. The commands are fixed and do not use shell=True, reducing injection likelihood.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes Macro File in User Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The script writes Module1.xba into the LibreOffice user macro directory. Persistent configuration writes are sensitive even when the macro content appears limited to recalculation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path construction and write operation are explicit. The risk is real because it changes user application state, although no malicious macro behavior was found.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often fails when formulas, formats, and calculated values drift out of sync. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through workbook creation, editing, analysis, and formula validation.
actual_capabilities: Create new XLSX workbooks with worksheets, formulas, formatting, and column sizing.
Read and analyze spreadsheet data using pandas workflows.
Modify existing workbooks while preserving formulas and formatting with openpyxl.
Apply financial model formatting conventions for inputs, formulas, links, and assumptions.
Recalculate formulas with LibreOffice and report spreadsheet error locations.
Check common formula issues such as broken references, division by zero, and invalid names.
limitations: Formula recalculation requires LibreOffice and a local Python environment.
The helper script modifies the local LibreOffice macro profile on first run.
Very large workbooks may take significant time to recalculate and scan.
It does not replace manual review for business logic or financial assumptions.

use_cases.title: Build an Analysis Workbook
use_cases.description: Create a clean workbook with imported data, summary tabs, formulas, and readable formatting.

use_cases.title: Update a Financial Model
use_cases.description: Modify an existing model while preserving layout, formulas, formatting, and source documentation.

use_cases.title: Validate Spreadsheet Outputs
use_cases.description: Recalculate a workbook and identify cells with formula errors before sharing the file.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel workbook from this table. Add headers, sensible widths, totals, and basic formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Spreadsheet Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Read this workbook, summarize each sheet, identify missing values, and create a clean analysis tab.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Modify this workbook using the existing formatting conventions. Preserve formulas and document any changed assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Build and Validate a Financial Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a multi-sheet financial model with assumptions, formulas, checks, formatting, and recalculated formula validation.

output_examples.input: Create a workbook that summarizes monthly sales by product and region.
output_examples.output: A formatted Excel workbook with raw data, summary tables, totals, percentage metrics, and formulas that update when inputs change.

output_examples.input: Review this model for formula errors after adding a new forecast year.
output_examples.output: A validation summary listing the workbook status, total formulas checked, and any cells containing spreadsheet errors.

output_examples.input: Clean this exported spreadsheet and make it ready for leadership review.
output_examples.output: A cleaned workbook with consistent headers, formatted numbers, adjusted column widths, and a concise summary sheet.

best_practices: Use formulas for calculated values so workbooks remain editable and auditable.
Recalculate and validate workbooks after adding or changing formulas.
Keep assumptions separate from formulas and document important data sources.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode calculated outputs that should update when inputs change.
Do not save workbooks opened with data-only mode when formulas must be preserved.
Do not run recalculation scripts on untrusted workbooks without local sandboxing.

faq.question: What file types does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Excel workbooks and tabular spreadsheet files, including XLSX, XLSM, CSV, and TSV workflows.

faq.question: Can it preserve existing workbook formatting?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance emphasizes matching existing templates and preserving established workbook conventions.

faq.question: Does it calculate formulas itself?
faq.answer: It writes formulas into the workbook and uses LibreOffice through recalc.py to update calculated values.

faq.question: Why does it need LibreOffice?
faq.answer: openpyxl can write formulas but does not evaluate them. LibreOffice provides local formula recalculation.

faq.question: Is it suitable for financial models?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes conventions for inputs, formulas, assumptions, source notes, and financial number formats.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: The recalculation helper runs local commands and writes a LibreOffice macro file into the user profile.
